children.heading: Short title
children.label: a Short title
children.text: This section may be cited as the “James Guelff and Chris McCurley Body Armor Act of 2002”.
nationally, police officers and ordinary citizens are facing increased danger as criminals use more deadly weaponry, body armor, and other sophisticated assault gear;
crime at the local level is exacerbated by the interstate movement of body armor and other assault gear;

there is a traffic in body armor moving in or otherwise affecting interstate commerce, and existing Federal controls over such traffic do not adequately enable the States to control this traffic within their own borders through the exercise of their police power;

recent incidents, such as the murder of San Francisco Police Officer James Guelff by an assailant wearing 2 layers of body armor, a 1997 bank shoot out in north Hollywood, California, between police and 2 heavily armed suspects outfitted in body armor, and the 1997 murder of Captain Chris McCurley of the Etowah County, Alabama Drug Task Force by a drug dealer shielded by protective body armor, demonstrate the serious threat to community safety posed by criminals who wear body armor during the commission of a violent crime;

of the approximately 1,500 officers killed in the line of duty since 1980, more than 30 percent could have been saved by body armor, and the risk of dying from gunfire is 14 times higher for an officer without a bulletproof vest;
the Department of Justice has estimated that 25 percent of State and local police are not issued body armor;
the Federal Government is well-equipped to grant local police departments access to body armor that is no longer needed by Federal agencies; and

Congress has the power, under the interstate commerce clause and other provisions of the Constitution of the United States, to enact legislation to regulate interstate commerce that affects the integrity and safety of our communities.

children.heading: Body armor
children.label: 1 Body armor
children.text: The term “body armor” means any product sold or offered for sale, in interstate or foreign commerce, as personal protective body covering intended to protect against gunfire, regardless of whether the product is to be worn alone or is sold as a complement to another product or garment.

children.heading: Law enforcement agency
children.label: 2 Law enforcement agency
children.text: The term “law enforcement agency” means an agency of the United States, a State, or a political subdivision of a State, authorized by law or by a government agency to engage in or supervise the prevention, detection, investigation, or prosecution of any violation of criminal law.

children.heading: Law enforcement officer
children.label: 3 Law enforcement officer
children.text: The term “law enforcement officer” means any officer, agent, or employee of the United States, a State, or a political subdivision of a State, authorized by law or by a government agency to engage in or supervise the prevention, detection, investigation, or prosecution of any violation of criminal law.

children.heading: Amendment of sentencing guidelines with respect to body armor
children.label: d Amendment of sentencing guidelines with respect to body armor

children.heading: In general
children.label: 1 In general
children.text: Pursuant to its authority under , the United States Sentencing Commission shall review and amend the Federal sentencing guidelines and the policy statements of the Commission, as appropriate, to provide an appropriate sentencing enhancement for any crime of violence (as defined in ) or drug trafficking crime (as defined in ) (including a crime of violence or drug trafficking crime that provides for an enhanced punishment if committed by the use of a deadly or dangerous weapon or device) in which the defendant used body armor.section 994(p) of title 28section 16 of title 18section 924(c) of title 18

children.heading: Sense of Congress
children.label: 2 Sense of Congress
children.text: It is the sense of Congress that any sentencing enhancement under this subsection should be at least 2 levels.

children.heading: Donation of Federal surplus body armor
children.label: f Donation of Federal surplus body armor

children.heading: Definitions
children.label: 1 Definitions
children.text: In this subsection, the terms “Federal agency” and “surplus property” have the meanings given such terms under .section 102 of title 40

children.label: A
children.text: is in serviceable condition;

children.label: B
children.text: is surplus property; and

children.label: C
children.text: meets or exceeds the requirements of National Institute of Justice Standard 0101.03 (as in effect on ).November 2, 2002

children.heading: Notice to Administrator
children.label: 3 Notice to Administrator
children.text: The head of a Federal agency who donates body armor under this subsection shall submit to the Administrator of General Services a written notice identifying the amount of body armor donated and each State or local law enforcement agency that received the body armor.

children.label: i
children.text: The Administrator of the Drug Enforcement Administration.

children.label: ii
children.text: The Director of the Federal Bureau of Investigation.

children.label: iii
children.text: The Commissioner of the Immigration and Naturalization Service.

children.label: iv
children.text: The Director of the United States Marshals Service.

children.label: i
children.text: The Director of the Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco, and Firearms.

children.label: ii
children.text: The Commissioner of U.S. Customs and Border Protection.

children.label: iii
children.text: The Director of the United States Secret Service.

children.heading: No liability
children.label: 5 No liability
children.text: Notwithstanding any other provision of law, the United States shall not be liable for any harm occurring in connection with the use or misuse of any body armor donated under this subsection.
